next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

The bar had high ceilings and the kind of exposed brick that meant it had been something else first. Doug counted fourteen beers on the chalkboard behind the counter while Simone ordered a bourbon neat. He picked one of the fourteen that sounded like a beer and not a statement, and the bartender, who had dark red nails and a silver ring on her thumb, poured it from a tap with a wooden handle.

Nine dollars. Doug did the math on what that would be annually if he came here twice a week, which was a thing he did with any purchase over six dollars. It was not a useful calculation. It was just what his brain did.

They sat at a high-top near the windows on tall stools that didn't have backs, which meant Doug had to decide what to do with his posture. He chose upright. Simone settled onto hers the way she settled into anything — as if she'd already assessed the structural integrity and found it adequate.

She didn't have her notebook.

Doug had been watching Simone write in that notebook for almost three months. She wrote in pencil, quickly, in handwriting that tilted so far left it looked like the words were leaning away from a strong wind. She wrote things Doug said when he didn't know he was saying anything worth writing. She underlined things twice. She crossed things out with single precise lines. The notebook had been on his kitchen table at every session, and now it wasn't here, and the absence registered the way a missing page in a claim file registered — not alarming, but indicative of a different kind of meeting.

It was the last Tuesday in May. The light...
open_hooks_to_carry: Cleveland: Doug overcompensates by imitating comedians — the costume is complete but dead; a woman yells 'Do the toast!' and Doug says 'I can't, I'm sober' — the funniest unintentional line of the night
Pittsburgh: Doug freezes for eleven seconds that feel geological — then the pool story works for ninety seconds before he loses the room again
prev_tail_excerpt:
...stared at the street with its one raised paw and the vacant lot next door was still vacant and the awning was still faded and everything was exactly where it had been when he'd pulled in an hour and a half ago, except that his daughter had driven two hours to tell him the worst thing she knew about him, and he'd sat there and taken it, and what she'd told him was true.

Doug started the car. He drove home to Clintonville. The route was familiar — right on High, left on Arcadia, past the library and the coffee shop and the intersection where a hydrant had burst last December and he'd assessed the resulting water damage to the duplex on the corner, four thousand in remediation, covered under the city's liability policy, closed and filed. He drove past it and the duplex looked fine. You couldn't tell anything had happened. The damage was repaired, the paperwork was complete, and the only evidence that fifteen hundred gallons of water had flooded the ground floor was in a claims file on a server somewhere, a record that documented everything that had been lost and everything it had cost to fix and said nothing at all about what it had felt like to stand in the water.

He parked. He went inside. He didn't eat cereal. He sat on the couch in the living room as the afternoon shifted into evening, and the apartment was quiet, and Simone's pen cap was still on the counter where he'd left it weeks ago, and the boxes were still in the second bedroom, and the water stain on the ceiling was still shaped like Ohio, and everything was where it had always been, and none of it was the same.
state_deltas_required: Three audiences in three cities expecting the toast guy and getting a man who can only be funny by accident in flashes he cannot control
The tour proves definitively that scripted Doug fails and unscripted Doug succeeds in uncontrollable flashes — Doug knows this now but cannot operationalize it, with the taping two weeks away
